name: Transport Layer
description: Quiz on control mechanisms, timing and transport protocols.

questions.question: TCP's 'three-way handshake' serves to synchronize the initial sequence numbers and allocate buffers on both hosts.
questions.explain: The SYN, SYN-ACK and ACK process establishes the parameters needed for a reliable connection before data is sent.

questions.question: UDP uses a 'sliding window' mechanism to avoid network congestion in video transmissions.
questions.explain: UDP has no native congestion-control or sliding-window mechanisms; it sends data at the rate the application provides.

questions.question: The 'EstimatedRTT' value is an exponentially weighted moving average that gives more weight to the most recent RTT measurements.
questions.explain: This lets TCP adapt smoothly to current changes in network conditions.

questions.question: The 'Checksum' field in the UDP header is mandatory on IPv4 networks to guarantee data integrity.
questions.explain: On IPv4, the UDP checksum is optional (although highly recommended and almost always used), unlike IPv6 where it is mandatory.

questions.question: TCP's 'Congestion Control' differs from 'Flow Control' because the former focuses on the network's capacity and the latter on the receiver's capacity.
questions.explain: Flow control protects the destination's buffer; congestion control protects the routers and links along the path.

questions.question: A 'Port' at the transport layer uniquely identifies a physical network interface (NIC) in the computer.
questions.explain: Ports identify processes or sockets within the operating system, not the physical hardware.

questions.question: In TCP, the 'Fast Retransmit' technique allows a segment to be retransmitted after receiving three duplicate ACKs, without waiting for the timer to expire.
questions.explain: This speeds up the recovery of isolated packet losses on high-speed connections.

questions.question: If a TCP host's receive buffer becomes completely full, it will advertise an 'rwnd = 0', forcing the sender to stop sending data.
questions.explain: This prevents the sender from continuing to send data that would necessarily be dropped by the receiver.

questions.question: The TCP protocol guarantees that, if a bit is altered during transmission, it will be corrected automatically without the need for retransmission.
questions.explain: TCP detects the error via checksum and drops the segment, requesting retransmission. It does not have Forward Error Correction (FEC) to correct bits.

questions.question: The UDP protocol is immune to denial-of-service (DoS) attacks because it keeps no connection state on the server.
questions.explain: Although it keeps no state, UDP is frequently used in amplification and flooding attacks; the lack of state does not make it immune.
